tribe_description: A tribe of pragmatic value gamers who judge products through a gaming and entertainment lens. They generally prioritize practical results, value for money.
tribe_definition: A tribe of pragmatic value gamers who judge products through a gaming and entertainment lens. They generally prioritize practical results, value for money.
population_definition: Gamers and software users who evaluate video games, mobile apps, and digital entertainment products.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They track post-launch support and drop recommendations as soon as patches stop or servers become unreliable.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Fitness-software and online-game gaps reveal reviewers prioritizing patch cadence and server uptime over first-day performance.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They view anti-consumer corporate moves-forced workaround apps, platform feuds, or account tie-ins-as critical flaws that outweigh technical merit.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Multiple gaps feature reviewers condemning bookmark-app workarounds and forced account integrations ahead of feature discussion.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They test every key function across all their household devices and blacklist any service that blocks even one major platform.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Streaming-app gaps show multiple reviewers stress-testing on every device and flagging platform lock-outs as first deal-breakers.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They A/B every product against a trusted reference device and reject anything that fails to beat the benchmark at its price.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Reviewers stacked gear against Xbox Elite controllers, Series X, or cheaper headsets before ruling on value.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They stick to proven, firsthand evidence and refuse to invent hypothetical flaws or untested risks when judging a product.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps reveal reviewers praising only what they experienced and ignoring predictions' made-up disconnect issues or usability fears.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They total up ownership costs, and mandatory accessories or future micro-transactions are deal-breakers even if the base price looks fair.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Users issued hard vetoes over proprietary memory cards, DLC paywalls, and surprise add-on costs.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: Any flaw in core reliability-battery, firmware, or build-triggers an instant "not recommended" regardless of specs or price.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Battery contacts, switch life, and firmware shutdowns caused immediate rejection across reviews.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: If the device feels uncomfortable or awkward on first contact, they stop testing and move on.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Multiple users aborted reviews when headsets or controllers failed their first-fit check.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They run through the entire real-world workflow-from unboxing to daily use-and quickly penalize any step that is confusing, slow, or awkward.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Evidence shows reviewers aborting over setup friction, large downloads, missing tools, and poor usability.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: They will overlook minor spec or price concerns when a product delivers a rare, unforgettable "wow" factor.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Several gaps show reviewers ignoring frame-rates and dollar-per-hour math once gameplay fun or immersion took over.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: A single breach of trust-privacy-invasive logins, censorship, or hardware failure-can push them to blacklist the entire brand or platform.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: Gaps cite forced Facebook accounts, content bias fears, and failed hardware as triggers for brand-wide bans.

main.text: They favor products that help build transferable real-world skills, not just momentary entertainment.
main.rationalization: Multi-user gaps show reviewers praising items as affordable educational stepping-stones rather than mere toys.

implicit_goals.text: They aim to build a high-performance gaming or tech setup without overspending.
They want to avoid frustration by choosing products that are reliable, comfortable, and light on DRM.
They hope to give the community trustworthy, data-backed buying guidance.

user_history_reviews.review_text:
I ended up returning this jacket, but i wanted to leave a review because it was actually such a nice coat.
The fabric is high quality, and feels great, large pockets, and when i put the coat on it fit, and looked great.
Im 6 foot 4, 300ish lbs, and finding a jacket that fits good, and has room but doesn't look baggy is very hard, and this fit the bill.
I ended up returning because its just too warm and heavy for me. I wish it was just the wool outside, but there's an insulated inside as well. I live in Tennessee, and while the winters get cool, this coat would still be overkill.
Highly recommend if want a nice looking jacket, and live in a cooler climate.
Note, i ended up buying the Dockers Weston Wool Jacket after returning this, also extremely nice. Its still a heavy coat, but much lighter than this one.

user_history_reviews.review_text:
After recently having eye surgery on both eyes, getting my lenses replaced, I know have perfect sight for once in my life. The slight down side to this is that after having surgery, my eyes are super sensitive to light in the short term. I can no longer wear my prescription shades so was looking for a stylish pair of shades that would both reduce the glare from the sun (I live in the South so there's a lot of sun) over the short term and also function over the longer term.

Looking around I came across these Bliz Fusion sunglasses. They are available in a selection of frame colors and tints. I went for the classic black frames with blue tint.

From the get go, the box itself is stylish. A good hefty black cardboard lined on the inside with a sort of suede to help stop getting the lenses scratched. Also included is another bottom fitting to the frames, in white and a spare nose piece, to give you option of having black upper frames with white lower. It may sound weird but this color combination actually works very well. The glasses also come in a very nice, almost silk feeling drawstring bag.

The glasses are quite sturdy with a straight leg. Very comfortable to wear with enough grip to keep them on your head whether your just strolling around or actually doing something physical like skiing or running. The lenses are 6cm tall with that wrap-around style. Not too big as to cover half your face but also not too small so that light leaks in.

Overall a very good and stylish pair of shades.

user_history_reviews.review_text: The material is very soft and nice, but the straps slide around a lot during any kind of rigorous movement like dancing. Even if you plan on mostly laying or sitting down in VR, the belt will constantly slide up and down your waist. Tightening the straps does not help this problem very much, even if you make them as tight as possible, because the material is simply too soft and thin.

I've really tried my best to make these things work, but at the end of the day they're just going to lightly tug your headset upward which is just awkward and annoying. They end up restricting your movement more than anything else, and tripping over your cord sounds preferable to feeling like the mask is being yanked upward all the time. It's pretty much impossible to go prone, and even squatting or moving a little too far away from the anchor point is uncomfortable. Despite my negative review, you might still try them anyways, because with something as niche as VR it's worth trying any method you can. Just keep in mind a ceiling suspension can feel very restrictive and uncomfortable.

I normally wear medium or large, I got 2XL (XXL) for the oversized look. It's comfortable, soft, nice and heavy, and has a really nice hood. The colors are nice and vibrant. The sleeves are WAY smaller than I expected, and I think it's too expensive, but overall I'm happy with the purchase.

If the creators made a version of this hoodie with extra-long sleeves, I'd buy it without hesitation.

The fabric is scratchy to the touch, and has terrible build quality. I normally wear medium or large, and I got XXL for the oversized look - and yet it was somehow still too small. The fabric is so thin and flimsy it's practically transparent. Even if this hoodie was free, I doubt anyone would want to wear it, not even as a lightweight warm weather hoodie. There are better options out there, don't waste your time with this one.

This is a good gaming or everyday mouse.  The honeycomb design makes it easy to grip and keeps you cooler during long sessions.  The mouse itself is pretty attractive, and I find that this unit does what it is supposed to do.  I had forgotten that it was rechargeable until it ran out of juice after several weeks of pretty consistent use.

Had a pair of old turtle beaches for the longest time but thought to myself what is the point in upgrading, my turtle beaches work. Well I am so pleased that I got these. They are more comfortable than the turtle beaches(don't hurt my ears after hours of play), they are lighter, and the sound quality is pretty stellar. Also when you aren't using them for gaming they are great for listening to music and getting some work done because they do offer fairly good noise cancellation. They are pretty big head phones, but that is the only downside I can think of. Despite them being big they are still light enough and easy to wear for hours at a time.

Overall for gaming or listening to music these are amazing.

leave_one_out_history_reviews.review_text: I have been using turbo tax every year for at least the last 10 years, except for the one year I gave H&R Block a try.  I ditched the  H&R Block and never used it again because it made it way to easy to miss deductions and to find out what you had missed.  There are a lot of things I love and hate about turbo tax, but mostly I like it.  The software interface and navigation is annoying, sometimes if I revisit a section it makes me answer the same questions again instead of remember that I had already answered all of that.  But what I like, is that I can dig down into the nitty gritty and see and compare literally everything about my taxes, as well as hop back and forth between sections if I want to (I tend to jump from topic to topic in the beginning to get the gist of whether I owe or not, then go back and revisit all the details of each topic).  The reports, comparisons, and online help are what really sets it apart. If there's a topic or dediction that I don't understand the rules of I can click help and immediately get a detailed explanationi of what it is.  So, as much as it has it's annoyances, I really can't imagine doing my taxes without it and will continue to purchase it year after year.

Love how easy it makes it to do my taxes, as well as being able to dig down into reports to see year by year comparisons. Hate that it always wants you to give them your personal info every time you open it so they can mail you marketing materials in the future. I mean really, I already bought their software, do they really need to send me more ads and stuff? I also wish they could find a way to streamline their code so that it would run a little quicker. I have a fairly new laptop with a ssd drive and it still runs pretty slow. But, since I haven't yet found a better alternative, I continue to use it year after year. I did try the H&R block tax cut one year, but it almost missed a key deduction and it was hard to go thru and compare to see what deduction was missing so I haven't used it again.

That said, don't buy this thinking it's going to feel simple just because it says Home & Student. The interface is still dense, with a lot of menus/panels, and if you're a first-time editor you're going to spend time hunting for things.

Once you get past that, the toolset is legitimately capable for flyers, logos, basic page layout, and cleaning up photos in PHOTO-PAINT. The templates/fonts/clipart are handy, but I'm not a fan of needing an online membership/account just to download some of the included content/updates. I want software I bought to work without extra hoops. Overall good value if you can handle the learning curve.

AHJ3S3V3XBCJZFF7EE4RALKBJPPQ.review_text:
My son is a computer geek and game player. He loves this stuff. Battlefield 4 was a Christmas gift. Since then, well, we haven't seen much of him. He's been locked up in his room playing this game.

According to him, you don't want the download game. Even with fiber optic broadband internet, this game would have taken ages to download. Moreover, with all the updates required to update the disc loaded game it still took quite awhile to become a playable game.

The have itself is excellent having had some time to cure the bus that plagued earlier generations of the game. The transitions, layers, sounds and movement are very good. The fun/wow factor is very high.

When a 17 year old computer dweeb says the game is fun, well folks, the game IS fun.
AHJ3S3V3XBCJZFF7EE4RALKBJPPQ.user_history_review:
First thing: disc vs digital. On my PS4 the disc still wasn't "pop in and play." It installed from disc, but I still had a big update before I could really jump into multiplayer. If your connection is decent you're waiting either way, so don't buy the disc thinking it skips the patch.

Once it's up and running, this is the kind of FPS chaos I love. The Frostbite engine makes the battles feel loud and alive, and the visuals hold up better than I expected. Controls are solid after a little tweaking, and the sound (especially explosions and gunfire) is a wow factor with a headset.

Multiplayer is the main reason to own it, but server consistency matters, and when it's flaky it kills the fun fast. When it's stable, though, Battlefield 4 is still a blast.

I'll be honest: my first hour with Sniper Ghost Warrior was me fighting the controls more than the enemies. The default mouse sensitivity felt "floaty" and the aim would overshoot, which is death in a sniper game. After some tinkering (lowering sensitivity, turning off a couple of the extra assists, and getting the scope feel dialed in), it finally clicked and I started getting that satisfying rhythm of lining up shots and clearing areas.

Once tuned, it runs fine on my older Windows box and the pack is a lot of game for the money. Graphics are decent for its age and the sound cues help. Just be ready to adjust settings before you judge it.

Overall it’s a fine game, just don’t get your expectations up.

AGCSHQRXX6N4TA2ZNC5BZHQ6PQYA.review_text:
I immediately switched to this controller from the xbox elite after that one started with really bad stick drift.
Going from one to another I could instantly notice the differences.
This controller is thicker in the handers, and where the paddles are is bigger as well.
With the elite, the paddles go right into the controller, with this there's a removable box you put the paddles in. The box makes the controller much bulkier.
The triggers and numbers are also not as good on this controller. The trigger lock, even on full lock, you have to pull down a good amount.

Good controller, not great. I also can't recommend the elite as it got crazy drift just 4 months after buying.

Coming from an Xbox Elite, this is about the same size but it feels a bit thicker with the back pro pack on. Weight is fine, but the paddles aren't as natural as the Elite's for my hands and I still get some accidental presses. Overall it's solid value if you want paddles on a budget, but ergonomics aren't quite "Elite level" for me.

AGG5VFUD4XK5TBLA4B724XEEV26Q.user_history_review:
Before anything: this is basically a mousepad with RGB, so the "software" part is the risk. On Windows, Synapse is still Synapse: wants to run in the background, start with boot, and it's chatty. You can make it behave (disable auto-start, block it in firewall) and the mat keeps the last lighting profile, but if you want zero extra services on a work machine, this is annoying for what should be a simple USB device.

Hardware wise it's thin and tracking is fine (no issues with my sensors), but the price is hard to justify vs a normal pad unless you really care about the lighting. For me it's OK, but I wouldn't recommend it for anyone trying to keep their PC lean/stable.

AEB5R4AU3242FXZFI6LJWQMYHKXA.review_text:
This is the new Oculus Quest 2, which is basically exactly the same as the older Quest except that it comes with half the memory, 128Gb of inbuilt storage rather than 256Gb and is priced $100 cheaper than the 256Gb version.

The Quest 2 is the perfect entry level headset to find out whether or not you like the VR world. Some people don't like VR for various reasons including vertigo and the feeling of getting sick, neck pain as the headsets are fairly heavy on your head etc. Other people love it and spend hours on it.

For your money your will get the headset itself, a fairly comfortable elastic strap to attach it to your head, two controllers and a charging cable. Setup is fairly simple. You connect it to your phone using bluetooth and just follow the prompts. You will need a Facebook account to tie it to and this puts some people off (I understand why) but the majority of people already have a facebook account anyway.

The Quest 2 has 2 1832x1920 LCD screens. Not quite 4K but a higher resolution than HD. For the money this isn't bad, especially considering the higher resolution Rift S is double the price. The eye pieces are slightly adjustable to 3 different positions, dependent upon the size of your head and space between your eyes. One great thing about the LCD display is that you can adjust the frame rate. I have mine set to 72Hz which I feel is much smoother than default 60Hz setting and this makes me much less prone to feeling sick. There is also a 90Hz refresh rate however for PC gaming that is too high unless you have a seriously beefy graphics card. Note however that you do not need a PC to use this.

The headset also has 3D capable audio built directly into the headset. The sound quality is OK but nothing outstanding. As such a lot of people also wear headphones when using the Quest 2 and there is a built in 3.5mm audio port to hook your headphones into. Personally, the sound is good enough for me and I prefer using it without the added weight of a pair of headphones.

The headset is quite heavy and although the soft elastic strap that comes with it is adequate, I would highly recommend purchasing one of the Elite, hard backed straps that you can find on amazon. These do make a lot of difference in distributing the weight, making wearing the headset much more comfortable and you can wear it for longer.

The Quest 2 has hundreds of apps and games available for it, from watching netflix and shooting games to fitness and boxing games. Even games such as boxing do help you to get in shape as you quickly find yourself ducking and weaving as well as throwing punches as you get immersed in the gameplay. These games are stored in the 128Gb of memory in the headset and aren't that large so even with 128Gb you should have plenty of room for a number of games and apps.

You can also attach your headset to your PC and play VR games on your PC. To do this you will need to buy an Oculus cable. The official one comes in at $80 which is very pricey. You can find third party cables that do the exact same thing for much less. I love to play DCS and Flight Simulator 2020 on my PC with the Quest. The immersion takes it to a whole other level, however in order to do so you will also need a fairly beefy PC to handle the graphics. My PC is a couple of years old, so its not powerful enough to turn up all the textures in Flight Sim to Ultra, but it is good enough to play with reasonably high definition. Honestly this is the main use I have for the Quest 2. At somepoint I will probably upgrade to the Rift S, but a new PC is in order before that.

So if your interested in dabbling with VR or don't have the budget to buy the Rift S then I would highly recommend the Quest 2. This 128Gb version, coming in at just $300 is well worth the money and I very highly recommend it.

AEB5R4AU3242FXZFI6LJWQMYHKXA.user_history_review:
This listing is basically the Beat Saber promo tied to buying/activating a new Meta Quest 2, so just know what you're actually paying for. On the Quest 2 side, the key specs are still the same: 1832×1920 per eye at up to 120Hz, and the lenses are decent once you take the time to dial in IPD and strap fit. Clarity is good in the sweet spot for rhythm games like Beat Saber, but it's not "edge-to-edge" sharp and you can get some glare if your fit is off.

Comfort is the make-or-break thing for me. The stock strap is fine for short sessions, but for anything longer I ended up needing a better strap (even a cheap halo style) and a different face pad to reduce pressure on my cheeks/forehead. Once I did that, Beat Saber sessions were way more enjoyable and the headset felt less front-heavy.

PC link is still a huge value multiplier if you have a gaming PC. Wired Link works reliably with a good cable, and Air Link can be great if your Wi‑Fi is solid, but it's more sensitive to your network than people admit. Having PCVR access is what keeps the Quest 2 from feeling like a closed toy, even though you're still dealing with Meta's ecosystem.

The promo itself is straightforward (no code), but the fine print is strict: you need a Meta account, you have to activate in the window, and you only get it if your account didn't already own Beat Saber. Also, don't forget the "additional music packs sold separately" part-Beat Saber can turn into ongoing DLC spend if you want specific tracks.

Overall, if you were already buying a new Quest 2, getting Beat Saber included is solid value. If you're trying to buy this as "just the game," this isn't that, and the account/eligibility restrictions are the main reason I wouldn't recommend it blindly.

The stock strap is fine for short sessions, but for anything longer I ended up needing a better strap (even a cheap halo style) and a different face pad to reduce pressure on my cheeks/forehead. Once I did that, Beat Saber sessions were way more enjoyable and the headset felt less front-heavy.

PC link is still a huge value multiplier if you have a gaming PC. Wired Link works reliably with a good cable, and Air Link can be great if your Wi‑Fi is solid, but it's more sensitive to your network than people admit. Having PCVR access is what keeps the Quest 2 from feeling like a closed toy, even though you're still dealing with Meta's ecosystem.

The promo itself is straightforward (no code), but the fine print is strict: you need a Meta account, you have to activate in the window, and you only get it if your account didn't already own Beat Saber. Also, don't forget the "additional music packs sold separately" part-Beat Saber can turn into ongoing DLC spend if you want specific tracks.

Overall, if you were already buying a new Quest 2, getting Beat Saber included is solid value. If you're trying to buy this as "just the game," this isn't that, and the account/eligibility restrictions are the main reason I wouldn't recommend it blindly.

leave_one_out_history_reviews.review_text:
This is the new Oculus Quest 2, which is basically exactly the same as the older Quest except that it comes with half the memory, 128Gb of inbuilt storage rather than 256Gb and is priced $100 cheaper than the 256Gb version.

The Quest 2 is the perfect entry level headset to find out whether or not you like the VR world. Some people don't like VR for various reasons including vertigo and the feeling of getting sick, neck pain as the headsets are fairly heavy on your head etc. Other people love it and spend hours on it.

For your money your will get the headset itself, a fairly comfortable elastic strap to attach it to your head, two controllers and a charging cable. Setup is fairly simple. You connect it to your phone using bluetooth and just follow the prompts. You will need a Facebook account to tie it to and this puts some people off (I understand why) but the majority of people already have a facebook account anyway.

The Quest 2 has 2 1832x1920 LCD screens. Not quite 4K but a higher resolution than HD. For the money this isn't bad, especially considering the higher resolution Rift S is double the price. The eye pieces are slightly adjustable to 3 different positions, dependent upon the size of your head and space between your eyes. One great thing about the LCD display is that you can adjust the frame rate. I have mine set to 72Hz which I feel is much smoother than default 60Hz setting and this makes me much less prone to feeling sick. There is also a 90Hz refresh rate however for PC gaming that is too high unless you have a seriously beefy graphics card. Note however that you do not need a PC to use this.

The headset also has 3D capable audio built directly into the headset. The sound quality is OK but nothing outstanding. As such a lot of people also wear headphones when using the Quest 2 and there is a built in 3.5mm audio port to hook your headphones into. Personally, the sound is good enough for me and I prefer using it without the added weight of a pair of headphones.

The headset is quite heavy and although the soft elastic strap that comes with it is adequate, I would highly recommend purchasing one of the Elite, hard backed straps that you can find on amazon. These do make a lot of difference in distributing the weight, making wearing the headset much more comfortable and you can wear it for longer.

The Quest 2 has hundreds of apps and games available for it, from watching netflix and shooting games to fitness and boxing games. Even games such as boxing do help you to get in shape as you quickly find yourself ducking and weaving as well as throwing punches as you get immersed in the gameplay. These games are stored in the 128Gb of memory in the headset and aren't that large so even with 128Gb you should have plenty of room for a number of games and apps.

You can also attach your headset to your PC and play VR games on your PC. To do this you will need to buy an Oculus cable. The official one comes in at $80 which is very pricey. You can find third party cables that do the exact same thing for much less. I love to play DCS and Flight Simulator 2020 on my PC with the Quest. The immersion takes it to a whole other level, however in order to do so you will also need a fairly beefy PC to handle the graphics. My PC is a couple of years old, so its not powerful enough to turn up all the textures in Flight Sim to Ultra, but it is good enough to play with reasonably high definition. Honestly this is the main use I have for the Quest 2. At somepoint I will probably upgrade to the Rift S, but a new PC is in order before that.

So if your interested in dabbling with VR or don't have the budget to buy the Rift S then I would highly recommend the Quest 2. This 128Gb version, coming in at just $300 is well worth the money and I very highly recommend it.

AHABUIZ7FQENNDSJUT6YBKEXX34Q.review_text:
The scroll-wheel is cheap and low quality, and already feels inconsistent after a few days of light usage. Double-clicking after just a few months. The firmware is very annoying, and constantly changes your DPI. Logitech support is horrible, so don't expect to be able to easily RMA. Unless you want to try to solder it yourself, you're probably just throw it in the trash and buy something else.

For the low price I wasn't expecting much, yet I'm still disappointed. The only positive thing I can say is that the design is nice and simple, but it's clearly for very small hands. I strongly recommend you avoid Logitech in general, since I've had very bad experiences with several of their mice.
AHABUIZ7FQENNDSJUT6YBKEXX34Q.user_history_review:
Mine started showing the usual "budget Logitech" problems way too fast. Scroll wheel got inconsistent (skips/doesn't register), and I've already had a couple moments that felt like early double-clicking. On top of that, G HUB is still a mess for something as basic as keeping DPI where I set it.

The shape is also on the small side. If you have average hands it feels a bit cramped over longer sessions, even though the weight is fine.

Yes, the sensor is solid for the price and the RGB is cute, but reliability is a core function for a mouse. Between the early hardware weirdness and the fact that dealing with support/RMA is never fun, I can't recommend it. I'd rather pay a bit more for something that lasts.

leave_one_out_history_reviews.review_text:
If you have VERY basic tax information and, for some reason, DON'T want to file state taxes, this is just about right for you. This is puzzling since I don't know anyone who DOESN'T have a need to file state taxes at least in my state, but if you're looking to shave a few dollars this is a safe bet.

TurboTax does a pretty good job of asking questions and helping to clarify some questions to make sure you understand the question being asked and even clarifying if some questions would be unusual if you answered "yes" to them.

If you do file state taxes and/or have a business, however, this is NOT the package for you.

Honestly I thought that I wasn't doing it right at first, but either the battery has unreliable contacts or doesn't hold a reliable charge.  I'd LOVE it if they worked, but I've finally given up on trying to make them work and feel like I shouldn't have to.  I can't recommend these.

AGAV7IY4HWGYRUIAGUZRXYUJ22DA.review_text:
My son has come a long way from his initial sheepish attempts at aviation on his phone to having a full set-up where he can really have an immersive experience. We love his interest in aviation and encourage his passion as he finishes school so we were thrilled to present this to him as an early Christmas gift.

Using Flight Simulator on a huge gaming monitor and/or his XBox really helps him develop his aviation faculties and at a fraction of the cost of flight training as he's still in university. LOL It is fairly simple to set up and connect the rudders/output source and is ready to get to work right away and you can also make simple adjustments to get it exactly to your optimal comfort. Response is precise with no glitching, mechanics are impressive and I can't recommend it enough for someone who loves aviation either as a hobby or wistful opportunity long gone or a budding captain of the sky. This is VERY fun and affordable and our wallets thank us!
predicted_themes: Hardware Build Quality Comfort and Connectivity
AGAV7IY4HWGYRUIAGUZRXYUJ22DA.user_history_review:
I picked this up as a relatively affordable "big gift" to feed my kid's flight sim obsession, and it's honestly one of those purchases that feels like a budget-friendly step toward real aviation skills compared to what even one intro lesson costs. Unboxing to flying was quick on both our Xbox Series X and my Windows 11 PC-plug it in, flip the input mode, and you're in.

The stick feels solid and comfortable in hand, and the control precision is the whole point here. The throttle/flap levers and the trim wheel make landing practice and small corrections way more "real" than a controller, and having lots of programmable buttons means you can actually learn proper workflows instead of pausing to hunt menus.

I'm also happy it doesn't force an account just to function. There is a companion app if you want to tweak things, but it's not a paywall. For the price, it's a huge upgrade in immersion and confidence-building, and I'd recommend it to anyone trying to take sim flying more seriously without overspending.

AGADSWBOE6IJXQKDYCFVVN7YBA2A.review_text:
I was skeptical in buying this game with all the negative reviews and low ratings. However I got it on a black Friday deal so I figured why not. I have played it for over a week now.

First off, I don't really know what people are crying about. The game is not that bad. in fact it is pretty fun. And I find it a lot like previous versions of fallout with minor differences
THINGS I HAVE NOTICED
1. The graphics look good. I play on a 65inch LG on an Xbox one S and I think the graphics are great. The open world looks awesome.
2. As many have griped about, there aren't any real NPCs in the game said from robots but who really cares.
3. You CAN attack other players once you reach level 5. However, you will get a bounty if that bothers you. Thankfully most of the community that I have come in contact with are pretty cool and don't mess with you too much.
4. when you die from another character you lose CAPS but not your gear which is cool
5. When you die from something else like a horde of super mutants that jump you (that happened), You loose your junk but not weapons armor and you have an opportunity to go retrieve it.
6. The open world is HUGE!!!! with a lot to explore. They have incorporated a C.A.M.P system which allows you to build a base wherever and as large as you want. However there are pros and cons to this
7. Despite what some individuals have complained about there are a lot of things in the game to do and lots that can kill you. but you are not bound to do anything but explore if you want

If you expecting to quickly unlock the best weapons and gear and go kill a lot of stuff then this game isn't for you.
As most have put it is more of a survival game. You must scavenge and craft (loot is plentiful) and modify weapons. You have to watch your food and water, health and Rads. There is a lot of missions and events but nothing as involved as previous versions of fallout. They still however, are entertaining.
ANNOYANCES
1. There are some minor glitches in the game like sometimes you may have to repeat pat of a quest that you already did.
2. The CAMP system can be cumbersome to use, and the method for saving your blueprints are kind of odd.
3. Collecting wood is annoying especially when I have a hatchet that I apparently cannot use on trees...really LOL
4. You seem to get hungry and thirsty ALOT which effects your AP.
Overall, I feel the game is worth buying, I don't know if I would of spent 65 bucks on it but it is definitely worth what I paid for it . Bethesda is actively patching and adding to the game so expect some large updates but this is a good thing --so be patient :) I did not give it 5 stars because it does have room for improvement but it is definitely not as bad as people make it out to be.
AGADSWBOE6IJXQKDYCFVVN7YBA2A.user_history_review:
I know Fallout 76 has a rough reputation and a lot of people still talk about it like it's an unplayable mess, but my actual experience on Xbox One has been way better than the internet version of this game. If you go in expecting "Fallout with friends" and a slower, loot/craft/explore loop (not a traditional single-player story dump), there's a ton here for the money.

Mechanically it's classic Bethesda in a good way: wandering Appalachia, grabbing junk, building out perks with S.P.E.C.I.A.L., and constantly getting distracted by locations. The West Virginia map is legitimately the star-different regions feel distinct, and it has that "one more objective" pull. CAMP building also ended up being more useful than I expected, not just cosmetic. Dropping a base, crafting, and using it as a fast hub makes the grind feel manageable.

Performance/bugs: I've had some jank (typical Bethesda stuff), but nothing that made it unplayable or wiped progress. Load times and occasional hiccups are a thing on older hardware, but in normal sessions it's been stable enough that I'm not rage-quitting.

The biggest asterisks are the always-online requirement and the monetization. You do need Xbox Live Gold, and that ongoing cost matters. Also, there are in-app purchases, which I'm never thrilled about even if you can ignore most of it. If you hate forced online games on principle, this won't change your mind.

Overall, at a decent sale price, I'd recommend it to anyone who wants a big open world to mess around in and doesn't need a pure single-player Fallout experience.

Angry Birds Space changes the way the game is played. They now give you a line to see the route the bird will take, but the game definitely isn't easier as they've complicated matters with things like gravitational field entry and exit (including orbit).

The visuals, the music and the gameplay are all big plusses here that bring something fresh to the Angry Birds title.

I love a good thinking game.

This one starts off really easy. You simply click a blue block and it disappears - letting the red blocks on top of it fall away and disappear. The green ones stay safe. As the levels progress, it gets significantly harder, requiring more thought and better timing to get rid of all the red blocks and keep the green ones. It's just like red remover if you've played that, but looks a bit more graphically polished on my kindle fire.

If you like skill and thought based games - give this one a try. It's worth checking out.

This mouse did not disappoint. It is well made using quality materials and you can feel that in the mouse. The cable is braided with a nice resistant nylon that will last for years of wear and tear.

I had previously installed the Razer management tool for previous keyboards and mice, so to get the mouse set up was a very simple process. If you have any experience with this tool then setting a custom color, pattern, and speed is a breeze. I will say though that the two buttons under the scroll wheel default to changing between the DPI setting profiles, these buttons are a little conspicuously placed and can be accidentally pressed causing you to lose some mouse control for a moment.

Beyond that one minor inconvenience with the button placement, the rest of the mouse feels and works great. It is beyond comfortable to use and you can feel the responsiveness in the clicks, buttons, and scroll. If you are looking for a new mouse without breaking the $100 price point, then this is a great mouse.

This keyboard is one of my favorite mechanical keyboards I have ever used. Within the past few years, I have used Razer's Black widow, a Ducky One 2, even Alienware's new product line of mechanical keyboards. Out of all of these mechanical keyboards, this has been the most compact, easy-to-use/intuitive, and satisfying keyboard.

The color range and configuration give you millions of options to customize your keyboard, and the configuration software UI is clean and runs without feeling clunky.

I have not been able to test this keyboards gaming ability, but the responsiveness with any other program has been phenomenal.

All in all this keyboard can hold it's own against the competitive mechanical keyboard market and shine!

AGU3LPNXOJ3LUHUNJD5QAZ3U563A.review_text:
I'm rather surprised this worked on my system. My gaming PC is an MSI GL63-series laptop with a Geforce 1660 TI Mobile graphics card. Not exactly the Lambo of gaming laptops or graphics, but it handles well enough for every game I've put it up against.
All the 'DisplayPort needed' talk made me wiggle too, but an MDP-to-DP adapter make it work perfectly. (Heck, it came with one in the packaging, too)

Pros:
So far, games work just fine on mediumish quality with little to no lag issues. I play Half Life: Alyx on Medium and it works very well, Beat Sabre give no trouble, and VRchat also operated quite well. The room-tracking actually impressed me, it was quite near 1:1 movement with maybe 2-15 ms delay at worst and that was because of the laptop most likely. It presented an appropriate sense of scale within applications, as well as ample crouch detection, which you need for some games behind cover!

The button configuration works well enough for all apps since SteamVR is handling most of the input mappings to known VR systems I suppose. Sure, it doesn't have a hand strap like the Index... so I use the wrist straps and don't drop my controllers. I don't hand per-finger tracking, but I got grips, and the interfaces are fine with that. Sure, I popped extra for the room sensors, it made logical sense so my clumsy self won't bump into the TV or couch.

Cons:
The cable can bunch up a bit after a few dozen turns around the room, I'll attest to that. Sort of wish the earphones would better press to my ears, but I can hear through them fine while they hover just a half-inch away from my ears. Wouldn't've minded a 3.5mm plug to auxiliary headphones. Mic works in VRchat clearly however.
Maybe this is just me still finding my VR legs, but after a few hours of usage, I do notice a 'tactile disparity' with the real world the next day. It's gotten better, probably need to tune that side eye-spacing knob a little better. But that knob ain't great and I can't go below 60mm which might help some smaller folk.
Sure I had to make a Vive account during setup but... at least it ain't hocking my personal information or locking my games to a Meta account like some VR solutions might be doing. I don't HAVE to use the Vive storefront; SteamVR interfaces with it just fine. In fact, it's what does the actual room setup.

Summary:
I mean, sure the Index would be baller, but some of us have more important things like... you know... rent or groceries or bills. Do you NEED a $1,000+ VR setup? ... I mean REALLY NEED it? Do you need a $1,000+ phone? I mean REALLY NEED it? Do you NEED a car with power windows or can you just use the hand crank to achieve the same outcome and save a couple hundred bucks?

Moderation in all things; the middle option is oftentimes sufficient. The HTC Cosmos Elite is plenty sufficient for its intended use case scenarios.

AGU3LPNXOJ3LUHUNJD5QAZ3U563A.user_history_review:
I bought the Vive Cosmos Elite because I wanted SteamVR lighthouse tracking without paying "latest flagship" money, and because inside-out tracking headsets I've tried always end up being the bottleneck once you start doing anything with hands behind your head (beatsaber crossovers, bow games, etc.). On that front, the external SteamVR tracking is the real reason to get this, and it delivers: once my base stations were mounted, tracking felt locked-in and consistent even with fast swings and awkward angles.

Visually it's a solid step up from older gen headsets I've used. The 2880x1700 panel looks sharp enough that I'm not thinking about screen door most of the time, and the sweet spot is decent once the fit is dialed. Comfort is... fine, but not "forget it's on" level for me. The crown-style strap makes it quick to put on and adjust, but the headset still feels a bit front-heavy over longer sessions unless you take the time to balance it right.

The integrated on-ear audio is convenient and good enough that I didn't immediately feel the need to strap on my own headphones. Not audiophile-level, but clean and usable for gaming.

Two big caveats for value shoppers: 1) lighthouse tracking isn't cheap in real life because you're committing to base stations and the whole ecosystem, and 2) setup is not "plug and play console simple." If you're the type who hates mounting hardware, dealing with room setup, and occasional SteamVR fiddling, that friction is part of the cost.

Overall I'd recommend it if you specifically want reliable SteamVR tracking and you're willing to pay (and tinker) for that. If you just want casual VR with minimal setup, there are cheaper/easier options that make more sense.
